- `Policy` cannot be None, but it's possible for us to trip over a NotFound exception here in a race condition.
- There will always be a `DefaultVersionId`. A policy is created with a version of v1, which is the initial `DefaultVersionId`. The `DefaultVersionId` can be switched between versions on the policy and there must always be one, and whichever version is designated as 'default' cannot be deleted. This ensures there is always at least one version and there is always a non-null `DefaultVersionId`.

docs for `frozendict` say: "Note that a frozendict does not guarantee the immutability of its values, so the utility of hash method is restricted by usage."

so, we'd probably have to roll our own safe `frozendict` impl to be able to reasonably rely on that. not opposed to it if we see more opportunities to utilize something like that, but flattening to tuples works fine for the immediate need here afaict.

I wasn't able to figure out how to do the automatic encoding of dict keys, because if they're contained inside a list, the default json encoding will take over (and raise a `TypeError`), unless you either (1) special-case a list with a single dict in it (2) override the private method used to encode dicts (3) re-implement the whole of the `json` module. In Python 3 mock is a built-in, and we're *almost* Python3-y.

Alright, here's the fixture for it. I'll go through it line by line:

We start with this minimum known fixture, based on what we found we needed in the test. The return line matches the left-hand-side of the assignment in the test exactly, same spelling and everything:

```python
@pytest.fixture
def index_fixture(self, request):
    return tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_
```

It returns the right items, and is minimal everywhere else, so this is strictly mechanical. This doesn't work because it doesn't have the return values defined anywhere, so we take them one by one, starting with tab_stops. We create an element from some compact XML (cxml) and construct a TabStops object from it. That's step two done.

```python
@pytest.fixture
def index_fixture(self, request):
    pPr = element('w:pPr/w:tabs/w:tab{w:pos=0}')
    tab_stops = TabStops(pPr)
    return tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_
```

For idx, we'll just say 0 here, since there's only one member, that's the only valid value.

```python
@pytest.fixture
def index_fixture(self, request):
    pPr = element('w:pPr/w:tabs/w:tab{w:pos=0}')
    tab_stops = TabStops(pPr)
    idx = 0
    return tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_
```

TabStop_ is a class_mock that's already defined, so we bring it in as a parameter of the fixture. py.test will automatically locate it and pass it into the fixture correctly when it instantiates the fixture.

```python
@pytest.fixture
def index_fixture(self, request, TabStop_):
    pPr = element('w:pPr/w:tabs/w:tab{w:pos=0}')
    tab_stops = TabStops(pPr)
    idx = 0
    return tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_
```

`tab` is that w:tab grandchild element of the w:pPr, so we get that using xPath:

```python
@pytest.fixture
def index_fixture(self, request, TabStop_):
    pPr = element('w:pPr/w:tabs/w:tab{w:pos=0}')
    tab_stops = TabStops(pPr)
    idx = 0
    tab = pPr.xpath('./w:tabs/w:tab')[idx]
    return tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_
```

And tab_stop_ is an instance_mock that's already defined among our fixture components, so we just add that as a parameter as well:

```python
@pytest.fixture
def index_fixture(self, request, TabStop_, tab_stop_):
    pPr = element('w:pPr/w:tabs/w:tab{w:pos=0}')
    tab_stops = TabStops(pPr)
    idx = 0
    tab = pPr.xpath('./w:tabs/w:tab')[idx]
    return tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_
```

Now this one should run fine, but we want a few more cases, so we parameterize the fixture:

```python
@pytest.fixture(params=[
    ('w:pPr/w:tabs/w:tab{w:pos=0}',                                 0),
    ('w:pPr/w:tabs/(w:tab{w:pos=1},w:tab{w:pos=2},w:tab{w:pos=3})', 1),
    ('w:pPr/w:tabs/(w:tab{w:pos=4},w:tab{w:pos=5},w:tab{w:pos=6})', 2),
])
def index_fixture(self, request, TabStop_, tab_stop_):
    pPr_cxml, idx = request.param
    pPr = element(pPr_cxml)
    tab = pPr.xpath('./w:tabs/w:tab')[idx]
    tab_stops = TabStops(pPr)
    return tab_stops, idx, TabStop_, tab, tab_stop_
```

And Bob's your uncle :)

`size` measures in Unicode characters for single byte encodings and wide characters, but in bytes for UTF-8.  This unfortunately means that the stemmer's behaviour is different for UTF-8 and non-UTF-8 versions.  To address this issue, we added `len` which always gives the length in Unicode characters, but that happened after you opened this PR so that wasn't available when you wrote this code.  I'm proposing to change this line to `$s = len` to get consistent behaviour regardless of the encoding in use.

> One might be to say roughly if [ ! -f $VENV_BOOTSTRAP_VERSION ]; then echo "$BOOTSTRAP_VERSION" | awk '{print $1, 1}' > "$VENV_BOOTSTRAP_VERSION"; fi;. Then PrevBootstrapVersion could just unconditionally check the file.

I don't think this has the behavior I described above (unless we make a rule that all new bootstrap scripts start at version 2).

For example, my plan was to split Red Hat based systems where we're installing a newer Python from EPEL into a new bootstrap script rather than adding a conditional to the existing RH script. The reason for doing this is it minimizes the number of people who need to rebootstrap.

If we always write "BootstrapFunc 1" if no file exists, some Red Hat users that need to rebootstrap won't do so. `certbot-auto` won't have created the file, we'll see it's the first version of the script (despite it now being a different script) and assume there's nothing to do.

My plan was to never modify this list. The existing bootstrap scripts are special because they existed before we saved the bootstrap version.

> There might need to be changes to how NO_BOOTSTRAP is handled; you wouldn't want that getting written to a file. And you wouldn't want it to re-bootstrap unnecessarily if BOOTSTRAP_VERSION was "NO_BOOTSTRAP 1" (because --no-bootstrap was passed) and then control hit if [ -d "$VENV_PATH" -a "$BOOTSTRAP_VERSION" != "$(PrevBootstrapVersion)" ]; then.

That's a good point. I think `--no-bootstrap` should just sidestep this logic entirely. In fact, it might be a good idea to copy over the version from the last bootstrap script we did run for when the user (possibly accidentally) runs without `--no-bootstrap` in the future.

> For new_order on an ACMEv1 client, we can pretty easily do the right thing: Turn it into a set of request_challenges. For request_challenges on ACMEv2 we could arguably synthesize an order out of them (now that you don't need a CSR to create an order), but it is probably better to just blow up.

Do we want to do this work though? My main concern here is making it clear to both existing and future ACME clients what methods they can and should use for each ACME version. I think separate classes (with a common base class containing most of the functionality) makes this self documenting. If you're using an ACMEv1 client, you can't call `new_order` because the method doesn't exists. Another example that presumably doesn't make sense is calling `agree_to_tos` on an ACMEv2 client.

If we don't go with the separate classes approach, we can try to document in the docstring for the class or each method what you can and can't use for each ACME version, but this to me feels awkward and less clear with the only major downside to the separate class approach being some initial refactoring up front.

This problem is actually why I've been pushing so annoyingly hard for autoformat everywhere, all the time. If we bit the bullet, formatted all the python code and read the ugly PR, and then put a pre-*commit* hook (we could copy [this script from go](https://tip.golang.org/misc/git/pre-commit)), no change in the codebase would ever introduce line noise, because it would always be formatted the same. Since we'd only ever format changed files, it'd be fast enough to be unnoticeable too. It could later open up the possibility of [mechanical source transformation](https://blog.golang.org/go-fmt-your-code).

A pre-push hook is a halfway measure, but is still far better than what's currently done. It also saves having to write overly complex scripts for what's really a problem with standards. 

30k lines of ray were autoformatted without any major issues. I think the rest of the python code could be done too. I'd be happy to review, since code formatting hardly requires deep knowledge of the actual code being run.

Any PRs against `master` could run yapf in their final commit and then only the diff would appear in the github PR review tool, so it wouldn't be a pain to review.

There would be one tricky thing to worry about: if a coroutine object gets `close`d or GCed before it even starts, then python doesn't enforce its normal guarantee that `finally` blocks will be executed. So if we did do this I guess we'd need to add another `yield` at the top of `step`, and immediately iterate it that far when we created the coroutine object, in order to guarantee that eventually `ag_running = False` would happen. you can still support both `*args` and `**kwargs`:
```python
def _retry_execute(self, func, *args, **kwargs):
   times = kwargs.pop("times", 3)
   ...
```
What I normally would do is to separate the special arguments in a decorator:
```python
def retry(times):
    assert times >= 1
    def wrapper(func):
        @functools.wraps(func)
        def wrapped(*args, **kwargs):
            ...
        return wrapped
    return wrapper
```
and of course then the call side becomes:

```python
@retry(3)
def download_model(...):
    ...

def _prepare_model_data(...):
    ...
    self.download_model(...)
    ...
```
